summary: DeVault explains how Go 1.11's module system finally resolved his longstanding frustration with GOPATH, which had kept him from adopting the language despite recognizing its potential. He argues that GOPATH crossed a line by imposing Go's opinions on his entire system rather than just his Go projects. With that obstacle removed, he finds Go's simplicity, concurrency model, standard library, and type system genuinely compelling. He praises Go modules as possibly the best module system in any programming language. While acknowledging complaints about error handling, the log module, and interface{}, he hopes Go will continue to prioritize simplicity over feature bloat. He closes by endorsing Go enthusiastically while cautioning against making it try to fill every niche.
key_points: GOPATH was the single biggest barrier to Go adoption because it imposed opinions beyond the Go project workspace onto the user's entire system
Go modules transformed DeVault's biggest complaint into one of his biggest compliments, demonstrating how solving the right problem can dramatically change perception
Go's conservative, simple language design produces remarkably consistent, high-quality code across the ecosystem, and most 'bad' Go libraries are ones fighting the language rather than embracing it
DeVault argues Go should resist pressure to add generics and complexity, warning that competing with Rust would ruin what makes Go great
A programming language that tries to fill all niches is a failed programming language
money_quotes: Go is opinionated, which is fine, but with GOPATH its opinions extended beyond my Go work and into the rest of my system.
I knew Go was cool, but it could be the second coming of Christ, and so long as it was annoying to use and didn't integrate with my workflow, I (rightfully) wouldn't care.
Go 1.11 took my biggest complaint and turned it into one of my biggest compliments.
Almost all third-party Go libraries are high quality stuff.
Most of the 'bad' Go libraries I've found are trying to work around Go's limitations instead of embracing them.

I'm not even slightly bothered by the lack of generics - years as a C programmer taught me not to need them, and I think most of the cases where they're useful are to serve designs which are too complicated to use anyway.
Go doesn't need to compete with anyone like Rust, and trying to will probably ruin what makes Go great.
A programming language which tries to fill all niches is a failed programming language.
key_insight: A language's developer experience and respect for existing workflows matters more than its technical merits — fixing Go's one overreaching design decision unlocked appreciation for everything else it got right.
